conf-proc-title-group.conf-normalized-title: Intelligent Systems: Theories and Applications (SITA), 2025 International Conference on

conf-meta.conf-name: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Systems: Theories and Applications (SITA)

title-group.article-title: Inventory Management Using Physics Informed Machine Learning

institution-wrap.institution: School of Engineering and Science, Indian Institute of Technology Madras Zanzibar

permissions.copyright-statement: 979-8-3315-5989-2 © 2025 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, and training of artificial intelligence and similar technologies. Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.

abstract.p: This paper presents a novel approach to inventory management by applying compartmental models based on differential equations across all three stages of the inventory lifecycle: raw materials, work in progress (WIP), and finished goods. Unlike previous studies that focus primarily on raw materials, our research provides a comprehensive framework for managing the entire inventory system. The proposed model effectively predicts inventory dynamics and optimizes costs while maintaining appropriate stock levels. This model enables more robust decision making in inventory management by unifying control theory and machine learning. It holds potential for realtime implementation in supply chains and can be extended to stochastic environments, perishable goods, and multi-echelon systems.

sec.label: I.
sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Inventory management is a critical component of supply chain operations, significantly influencing organizational performance and profitability [2], [19]. Traditional inventory management methods often emphasize individual stages in isolation, rather than adopting a holistic view of the entire system [20]. This research proposes a comprehensive framework that models all three key stages of inventory, raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), and finished goods, using compartmental models formulated through differential equations, combined with artificial intelligence techniques.

Originally developed in the field of epidemiology [1], compartmental modeling has demonstrated considerable potential in supply chain applications as well [18]. Recent advancements in neural networks and optimal control theory have enabled the development of more sophisticated approaches for inventory optimization [16]. Building upon these developments, our study addresses several gaps in the existing literature by integrating control-theoretic and machine learning perspectives.

Specifically, our approach leverages Pontryagin's Maximum Principle (PMP) [14] in conjunction with neural networks to derive optimal inventory control strategies. While prior research has primarily applied Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to the management of raw material inventory [8], our work extends the application of PINNs to all stages of the inventory lifecycle. This broader scope offers a more unified and effective method for managing inventory across the supply chain.

The key contributions of this work include:
•
A novel compartmental model framework that captures the dynamics of all three inventory stages simultaneously. •
Integration of Pontryagin's Maximum Principle with neural networks for inventory optimization. •
Comprehensive numerical validation demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method.

This paper is organized as follows. Section 2 presents the mathematical formulation of our compartmental inventory model. Section 3 details the optimization framework and Pontryagin's approach. Section 4 describes our numerical methods and presents results. Finally, Section 5 concludes with implications and future research directions.

sec.label: II.
sec.title: Model Formulation
sec.p:
Our research introduces an Artificial Neural Network (ANN)-based Decision Support System for optimizing inventory management through compartmental modeling. This approach treats inventory as a system of interconnected stocks whose dynamics are governed by differential equations, similar to models used in epidemiological studies [4] but adapted for supply chain applications [6].
Fig. 1.
Inventory model.

Figure 1 illustrates a three-stage inventory flow system commonly found in manufacturing and supply chain operations.
•
Raw Materials: These are the basic inputs required for production. Procurement decisions (governed by control variable $\lambda_{r}(t)$ determines how much raw material is added to the system. •
Work in Progress (WIP) Raw materials are processed through manufacturing operations to become intermediate products. Transformation rates govern this stage, $\mu_{R}$ and $\mu_{W}$. Depicted by interconnected gears, symbolizing machinery and production complexity. •
Finished Goods The final products are ready for customer delivery or sale. The sales or dispatch rate is controlled by $\lambda_{r}(t)$.

sec.label: A.
sec.title: Inventory of Raw Materials

sec.label: B.
sec.title: Inventory of Work-in-Progress (Wip)

sec.label: C.
sec.title: Inventory of Finished Goods

sec.label: III.
sec.title: Optimization Problem
sec.p: Our optimization framework is inspired from Pontryagin's Maximum Principle (PMP) [11] to minimize total inventory costs while maintaining appropriate stock levels. The model incorporates three primary cost components.

We apply the Pontryagin's Maximum Principle [3] assuming constant rates for $\mu_{R}, \mu_{W}$, and $\lambda_{F}$ to simplify the control problem while maintaining the integrity of the model. Here, the control variables are $\lambda_{r}(t), \mu_{r}(t), \mu_{w}(t), \lambda_{f}(t)$, and we assume that $h_{R}, h_{W}, h_{F}$ are constant.

In the framework of optimal control theory, particularly under Pontryagin's Maximum Principle, the co-state equations govern the evolution of the adjoint variables, also known as Lagrange multipliers or co-state variables. These variables are denoted as $\psi_{R}(t), \psi_{W}(t)$, and $\psi_{F}(t)$ are introduced to account for the influence of the state variables (e.g., inventory levels of raw materials, work-in-progress, and finished goods) on the total cost function. In practical terms, a co-state variable represents the shadow price or sensitivity of the system, it quantifies how much the total cost would change in response to a small variation in the corresponding state variable.

sec.label: IV.
sec.title: Parameter Estimation
sec.p:
We employed a range of methods to analyze and validate our inventory management system, drawing on recent advances in scientific machine learning [12]. Specifically, we implemented the Runge-Kutta method [5] with the following parameters:
•
Initial time: $t_{0}=0.0$ •
Initial raw materials: $R_{0}=100$ •
Initial work-in-progress (WIP): $W_{0}=40$ •
Initial finished goods: $F_{0}=10$ •
Time step: $h=0.1$ •
Number of steps: $n=100$

Figure 2 presents the numerical simulation results of the system dynamics using the Runge-Kutta method. It illustrates the evolution over time of the three main inventory components:
•
$R(t)$ - Raw Materials (Blue Curve): Initially starts at a high level and decreases over time due to consumption in the production process. •
$W(t)$ - Work-in-Progress (Orange Curve): Increases initially as raw materials are converted into intermediate products. It eventually stabilizes, indicating a balance between incoming and outgoing flow. •
$F(t)$ - Finished Goods (Green Curve): Grows gradually as WIP is processed into final products ready for sale.
Fig. 2.
Solution of the system of inventory management equations using the runge-kutta method.
This simulation validates the system's dynamic behavior governed by the differential equations.

We also employed a neural network architecture consisting of three hidden layers, each with 20 neurons and tanh activation functions, following the design proposed in [10]. The parameters used in the model were set as follows:
•
$\lambda_{r}(t)=1.0$ •
$\mu_{r}(t)=0.1$ •
$\mu_{w}(t)=0.05$ •
$\lambda_{f}(t)=0.02$
Fig. 3.
Solution of the inventory management system using physics-informed neural networks (pinns)

The system reaches steady state behavior after a short time, where all inventory levels become stable as shown in Figure 3. PINNs successfully capture the dynamics without requiring large amounts of labeled data. The inventory of raw materials starts at zero and steadily increases, eventually saturating. This suggests the model learns to procure raw materials at a growing but stabilizing rate, as shown in Figure 4. The WIP inventory grows gradually over time, fed by the increasing raw materials and processed through internal production. Finished goods begin with negative values (due to model initialization) and increase slowly, representing the final output stage of the inventory system. The smooth and converging trends indicate that the PINN has successfully captured the dynamic inventory flow. Unlike classical methods (e.g. Runge-Kutta), PINNs do not need labeled solutions, they learn directly from the governing differential equations and constraints. These results show stability and learning of long-term behavior, making PINNs a reliable tool for complex inventory optimization systems.
Fig. 4.
Temporal dynamics of inventory levels in the raw material, work-in-progress, and finished goods compartments.

sec.title: Computing Costs:
sec.p:
Several cost components were analyzed following the framework presented in [15]:
•
Holding Cost: Calculated as the cumulative cost of holding inventory across all three components (raw materials, WIP, and finished goods). •
Procurement Cost: Proportional to the rate of raw material procurement. •
Production Cost: Dependent on the production rate and the level of work-in-progress. •
Total Cost: Computed as the sum of all individual cost components.

The results presented in Figure 5 illustrate how inventory transitions through the various supply chain compartments over a 10 -unit time horizon. Raw materials, which begin at a high level of 100 units, decrease rapidly as a result of consumption in the production process. This sharp decline reflects an intensive procurement-to-processing phase with no replenishment.

Starting from zero, the work-in-progress (WIP) inventory increases rapidly as raw materials are converted into intermediate products, reaching a peak around $t=2.5$. Thereafter, it declines steadily as the WIP is transformed into finished goods. Finished goods inventory also starts at zero and gradually accumulates as production output increases. The red curve, which represents finished goods, peaks near $t=4.5$ and subsequently decreases as the rate of sales or depletion exceeds the rate of production.
Fig. 5.
Inventory dynamics across various supply chain compartments are observed over a 10 -unit time horizon.
Fig. 6.
Temporal dynamics of major cost components in the inventory management system under optimal control strategies.

Figure 6 illustrates the time evolution of key cost components in the inventory management system governed by optimal control strategies.
•
Holding Costs: Represent the cost of storing raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), and finished goods. These costs peak around $t=2$, indicating maximum inventory levels, and then gradually decline as inventory decreases over time. •
Procurement Costs: Associated with the rate of acquisition of raw materials, $\lambda_{r}(t)$. These costs start high, then drop, and stabilize, reflecting an efficient procurement strategy implemented early in the time horizon. •
Production Costs: Dependent on the rate of WIP transformation, modeled as $\mu_{w}(t) \cdot W(t)$. These costs increase rapidly, peaking around $t=1.5$, and then slowly decline. This captures the dynamics of the production effort over time. •
Total Costs: Computed as the sum of all three components:
Total Cost = Holding Cost + Procurement Cost + Production Cost.
Initially, the system experiences high total costs due to rapid procurement and production ramp-up. Over time, costs decrease as inventory is optimally depleted and production activities taper off. This trend validates the effectiveness of the applied optimal control strategies, demonstrating successful cost minimization over the time horizon.
Fig. 7.
Evolution of cost components over successive replenishment cycles in a periodic inventory policy framework.

Parameters included: Cycle time: 5 units, Pulse duration: 0.1 units, Raw material input rate $\lambda_{r}$: 100 units per cycle, Conversion rates $\mu_{r}, \mu_{w}, \mu_{x}: 0.8,0.8$, and 1.2, respectively Cyclic Restocking: Every 5 units of time, the system restocks raw materials, visible as sharp jumps in the blue line as shown in the Figure 8, while this periodic replenishment triggers a cascade of production across compartments.

Exponential Depletion: After each restock, raw materials are consumed first, decreasing exponentially. This drives work-in-progress (WIP), which also depletes gradually. Finished goods accumulate last, then reduce as sales happen. The three compartments follow first-order decay curves between restocks. Inventory flows maintain synchrony, showing a stable conversion process:
Raw Materials $\quad \xrightarrow{\mu_{r}} \quad$ Work in Progress (WIP) $\quad \xrightarrow{\mu_{w}}$ Finished Goods.

This system mimics batch manufacturing or periodic procurement, useful for: seasonal industries, production with scheduled delivery, and optimized warehouse space usage. The results highlight the effectiveness of using optimal control $\lambda_{r}$, $\mu_{r}, m u_{w}$, and $\lambda_{f}$ to minimize cost, maintain service levels, and prevent overstock or stockout.

The Dispatch Rate is constrained by actual market demand as shown in Figure 9. The Raw Materials and WIP inventories adapt to maintain production continuity. Inventory accumulation or depletion is influenced by the time-varying demand. This simulation demonstrates a realistic production-dispatch scenario, where the inventory levels are regulated dynamically to match fluctuating demand while minimizing holding costs and avoiding overproduction.
Fig. 8.
Trajectory of inventory levels over time under a periodic replenishment policy, illustrating the cyclical pattern of stock depletion and replenishment across planning periods.
Fig. 9.
Inventory dynamics with demand constraint: time evolution of raw materials, work-in-progress, finished goods, and customer demand. the plot demonstrates the interaction between inventory levels and periodic demand fluctuations over time.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This study introduced a novel AI-augmented optimal control framework for inventory management, addressing the dynamic interactions among raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), and finished goods. Simulation results revealed that our proposed method effectively stabilizes inventory trajectories, even under constraints like periodic replenishment and variable demand. The approach achieved superior cost efficiency compared to traditional methods, demonstrating strong generalization in approximating optimal state and control functions.

This work bridges the gap between classical control theory and modern AI techniques, offering a scalable framework for real-time inventory optimization. Future extensions may include incorporating uncertainty, multi-echelon networks, perishability, and stochastic demand to enhance applicability in complex supply chain environments. Future research directions include:
•
Extension to multi-echelon supply chain networks •
Incorporation of stochastic demand patterns •
Application to perishable inventory systems •
Real-world validation with industrial partners

sec.label: V.
sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: This study uses solely synthetic data, which was generated for the purpose of this research.

ref.mixed-citation: Anderson, R. M., & May, R. M. (1991). Infectious diseases of humans: dynamics and control. Oxford university press.
Axsäter, S. (2015). Multi-Echelon Systems: Structures and Ordering Policies. In Inventory Control (pp. 147–170). Cham: Springer International Publishing.
Bertsekas, D. (2012). Dynamic programming and optimal control: Volume I (Vol. 4). Athena scientific.
Brauer, F., Castillo-Chavez, C., & Feng, Z. (2019). Mathematical models in epidemiology (Vol. 32). New York: Springer.
Butcher, J. C. (2016). Numerical methods for ordinary differential equations. John Wiley Sons.
Disney, S. M., & Towill, D. R. (2006). A methodology for benchmarking replenishment-induced bullwhip. Supply Chain Management: An International Journal, 11(2), 160–168.
Harris, F. W. (1990). How many parts to make at once. Operations research, 38(6), 947–950.
Karniadakis, G. E., Kevrekidis, I. G., Lu, L., Perdikaris, P., Wang, S., & Yang, L. (2021). Physics-informed machine learning. Nature Reviews Physics, 3(6), 422–440.
Kogan, K., & Tapiero, C. S. (2007). Supply chain games: operations management and risk valuation. Boston, MA: Springer US.
Lagaris Lagaris, I. E., Likas, A., & Fotiadis, D. I. (1998). Artificial neural networks for solving ordinary and partial differential equations. IEEE transactions on neural networks, 9(5), 987–1000.
Liberzon, D. (2011). Calculus of variations and optimal control theory: a concise introduction. Princeton university press.
Lu, L., Meng, X., Mao, Z., & Karniadakis, G. E. (2021). DeepXDE: A deep learning library for solving differential equations. SIAM review, 63(1), 208–228.
Nahmias, S., Cheng, Y. (2009). Production and operations analysis (Vol. 6). New York: McGraw-hill.
Pontryagin, L. S. (2018). Mathematical theory of optimal processes. Routledge.
Porteus Porteus, E. L. (2002). Foundations of stochastic inventory theory. Stanford University Press.

Raissi, M., Perdikaris, P., & Karniadakis, G. E. (2019). Physics-informed neural networks: A deep learning framework for solving forward and inverse problems involving nonlinear partial differential equations. Journal of Computational physics, 378, 686–707.
Richards, G. (2017). Warehouse management: a complete guide to improving efficiency and minimizing costs in the modern warehouse. Kogan Page Publishers.

Sourirajan Sourirajan, K., Ozsen, L., & Uzsoy, R. (2009). A genetic algorithm for a single product network design model with lead time and safety stock considerations. European Journal of Operational Research, 197(2), 599–608.
Wild, T. (2017). Best practice in inventory management. Routledge.
Zipkin, P. H. (2000). Foundations of inventory management mcgraw-hill. Irwin, New York, USA.
